title: Installing a field gate for livestock
subtitle: Posts, hinges, and a closing chain that actually holds.
excerpt: A gate that sags, swings open, or needs two hands and a knee to close is a constant frustration. Getting the posts right is the whole job: once they are in straight and at the right depth, the gate hangs true and stays that way for years. A step-by-step guide to setting the hanging post, setting the slamming post, and hanging a standard five-bar gate.
sourceNotes: John Seymour, The New Complete Book of Self-Sufficiency (2003), fencing and gatework chapter. SAC Consulting Scotland, farm infrastructure guidance. AHDB Beef and Lamb, farm safety and animal handling facilities.

glossaryTerms.definition: The post that carries the weight of the gate via the hinges. Must be set deeper and more solidly than the slamming post because it bears all the leverage load when the gate is open.
The post that the gate closes against. Carries the gate catch or chain. Needs to be plumb and at exactly the right distance from the hanging post to match the gate width.
A short length of chain attached to the gate and looped over the slamming post or catch. The simplest and most reliable field gate fastening for everyday use with livestock.

content.text: A field gate is only as reliable as its posts. The most common gate problem on a smallholding is not a broken gate but posts that have moved: a
hanging post
that has shifted outward will sag the gate; a
slamming post
that has moved will stop the gate from closing. Getting both posts set plumb and at the correct spacing before hanging the gate is the whole job.
Choosing the post material

Gate posts for livestock work are typically 150 mm x 150 mm treated softwood (a minimum), 200 mm x 200 mm for a wider or heavier gate, or a 125 to 150 mm diameter round treated hardwood post. The total post length should be the gate height plus at least 900 mm for burial depth, with an extra 50 mm for the post to stand proud of the ground. A 1.2 m gate needs a post at least 2.1 m long.
Concrete-in hanging posts will last longer and hold straighter. Drive-in posts are faster but the hanging post will need checking and re-driving every few years on soft ground.
Setting the hanging post

Mark the hole position. For a driven post: use the post rammer to drive the post to the correct depth, checking for plumb on two faces as you go. Check on both the face toward the gate and the face perpendicular to it; a post that is plumb in only one plane will still sag the gate.

For a concreted post: dig a hole at least 900 mm deep with a post-hole spade or bar. Set the post in the hole, pack around the base with a layer of aggregate, and check plumb before pouring concrete. Use a stiff, dry concrete mix rather than a wet pour; a dry mix sets around the post without running away from it. Allow at least 48 hours before attaching the gate.

attrs.title: Set the hanging post on the uphill side
attrs.body: If the gateway is on a slope, the hanging post goes on the uphill side. An uphill-hung gate swings open and stays open under gravity, which is useful when moving stock through. A downhill-hung gate falls shut on its own, which is a nuisance in a handling system. Level gates on flat ground can hang either side; convention places the hinge post on the left as you approach from outside the field.

content.text: Setting the slamming post

The spacing between the inside face of the hanging post and the inside face of the slamming post must match the gate width exactly, less 30 to 50 mm for the latch engagement. Measure your gate before setting the slamming post. A gap that is too wide leaves the gate loose on the latch; a gap too narrow prevents it from closing.

Set the slamming post to the same depth as the hanging post and check plumb on two faces. It carries less load than the hanging post but must be immovable; livestock pushing against a gate apply significant force to the slamming side.
Hanging the gate

Hang the gate with two people if possible: one to hold the gate at height while the other fits the hinge pins. Hook the bottom hinge onto the lower pin first. The bottom hinge bears most of the weight; fitting it correctly prevents the gate from being twisted out of alignment as the top hinge goes on.

Open and close the gate several times after hanging. It should swing freely without dragging on the ground and should sit square in the frame when closed. A gate that drags immediately after hanging either has the hinges at different heights, or the ground is too high directly under the gate line. Trim the ground if needed rather than adjusting the hinges.
Fitting the fastening
A
gate chain

looped over a post or hook is the most reliable fastening for a working field gate. It is one-handed to operate, visible from a distance, and cannot be accidentally left open the way a spring latch can. Attach the chain to the gate post with a staple or gate eye, at a height you can reach from horseback or from an ATV if needed.

items.cause: Hanging post not set deep enough, or set into soft ground without concrete
items.fix: Drive the post deeper or excavate and concrete in. A temporary repair is a prop strut nailed to the hanging post at 45 degrees and buried, but this is not a long-term fix.

items.symptom: Gate swings open or closed on its own
items.cause: Post is not plumb; the gate is hanging on a lean that tilts it in one direction
items.fix: Check the post with a spirit level on two faces. If the post is out of plumb, it needs to be straightened before the gate will hang true. A concreted post can sometimes be corrected with a steel bar as a lever within the first week before the concrete fully sets.

items.symptom: Gate cannot be closed flat against the slamming post
items.cause: Post spacing too narrow, or hinge pintle angles causing the gate to cant inward
items.fix: Check post spacing against the gate width. If the spacing is correct, examine the hinge pintles: they may need bending slightly to adjust the swing angle.
